query: distal incision 3rd molar extraction lingual nerve

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Technique or Treatment -- Flap Design
retrieved_docs.content: Most importantly, the lower third molar is closely situated to the lingual nerve, which is protected only by thin mucosa on the mesial aspect of the mandible. A two-sided or three-sided flap, also known as Ward's incision, is the ideal design of choice, with the distal relieving incision positioned buccally to avoid damage to the lingual nerve. The distal relieving incision is made 45 degrees to the distobuccal cusp of the lower third molar.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Mandibular third molars are the most frequently extracted teeth, accounting for 18% of dental extractions. [1] [2] Third molars typically develop around the age of 8 to 15 [3] and erupt between the ages of 17 to 22. [4] As a result of this delayed eruption, mandibular third molars are often impacted, with 17 to 69% presenting with some degree of impaction. [5] Extraction of third molars is further complicated by its anatomical proximity to the inferior alveolar nerve (IAN), the third branch of the fifth cranial nerve, which supplies sensory function to the lower cheek, chin, lip, tongue, gingivae, and teeth. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Oral paraesthesia as a result of third molar surgery is preventable in most cases. Thorough assessment and surgical planning are important steps to minimize the possibility of inferior alveolar or lingual nerve damage. Although relatively uncommon, IAN or lingual nerve paresthesia can profoundly affect a patient's quality of life. As of yet, very few cases report complete resolution of permanent paraesthesia. As a result, good knowledge of head and neck anatomy, surgical technique, and pre-operative planning are critical to successful patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Technique or Treatment -- Flap Design
retrieved_docs.content: Previously, lingual nerve protection by way of raising a lingual flap and retraction of the lingual tissues with a Howarth's or similar was practiced to prevent damage to the lingual nerve. Blackburn (1989) demonstrates that this increases the risk of lingual nerve damage and, as such, is no longer indicated. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lingual Nerve -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The lingual nerve is one of the two nerves most commonly injured during oral surgery with the other being the inferior alveolar nerve [3] . Numerous procedures have been thought to cause lingual nerve damage, including mandibular resection, extraction of a third molar tooth, surgery of the salivary glands, excision of tumors, and lingual flap retraction [7] [8] .

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Complications -- Inferior Dental Nerve Injury
retrieved_docs.content: This risk usually pertains to mandibular third molars, although mandibular second molars can occasionally present close to the IDN. The patient should always be warned of the risk of temporary or permanent altered sensation to the lip, cheek, tongue, and teeth on the relevant side. The incidence of IDN injury is between 0.35% to 8.4%, with a permanent injury to the nerve being rare. [58]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Complications -- Nerve Injury
retrieved_docs.content: IAN and lingual nerve injury can complicate the extraction of impacted mandibular 3rd molars. Damage to these nerves can result in temporary or permanent paresthesia. Paresthesia is relatively rare but may arise as a temporary condition. [40] A patient with a nerve injury should be closely followed up. Recovery from an IAN injury starts at around 6 to 8 weeks, but full recovery may take up to 2 years. [41] Local anesthesia injection can also cause nerve injuries. Neurotoxicity, rather than direct needle trauma, is believed to contribute more significantly to injection injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lingual Nerve -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: An increase in the number of second molar implants has also increased the risk of injury to the nerve [3] . However, the lingual nerve most commonly gets damaged during inferior alveolar nerve block injections and third molar tooth removals [3] [10] . Reports indicate that damage to this nerve occurs during the removal of the third molar between 0 and 23% of the time [11] [12] . The wide variation in reported frequencies of this injury may be in part due to the variations seen in the course of the lingual nerve. Potential mechanisms for preventing lingual nerve injury during third molar surgery include avoiding lingual flap elevation and conducting tooth sectioning [8] [13] .

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Maxillary posterior teeth can even be displaced as far posteriorly as the infratemporal fossa, often requiring alternative surgical approaches for retrieval. [32] The displacement and even the retrieval of roots from any anatomic space in the head or neck can increase patient morbidity. [32] Best practices involve only attempting retrieval if the tooth or root is fully in-view and easily graspable at displacement time. Otherwise, an immediate specialist referral for evaluation and treatment is the prudent approach. [29] Iatrogenic nerve injury is another rare but serious complication associated with tooth and root removal, particularly with mandibular third molars. In some patients, the roots of the mandibular third molar can be in direct contact with the mandibular canal that houses the IAN. One study showed that the frequency of intimate positioning between the mandibular third molar roots and the mandibular canal was as high as 7.1%. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Using a 15-blade and a periosteal elevator, a sulcular incision is created with appropriate releasing incisions to raise a subperiosteal flap of adequate size to allow visualization, access, and delivery of the tooth. When creating a flap for extracting a 3rd molar, a distal release incision toward the lingual aspect of the mandible should be avoided, as the lingual nerve may lie on the alveolar bone in this location. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The mandibular nerve is the third and largest division of the fifth cranial nerve, known as the trigeminal nerve. It innervates the skin of the lower face and lip, the muscles of mastication, the mandibular dentition, gingivae, and the anterior two-thirds of the tongue. It supplies both sensory and motor function. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The surgical handpiece used for bone removal must exhaust air pressure away from the surgical site to avoid air embolism or tissue emphysema. Lingual bone removal must be avoided to prevent injury to the lingual nerve when extracting mandibular teeth. A common practice after exposing the tooth is to section it to facilitate removal. The tooth is typically sectioned 3/4 of the way toward the lingual aspect using the surgical handpiece and bur, and the remaining union is split using an elevator. The surgical bur is placed parallel to the buccal groove in impacted molars.

retrieved_docs.title: Inferior Alveolar Nerve and Lingual Nerve Injury -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mandibular third molar extraction is the leading surgical cause of IAN and LN injury, but rates fluctuate with operator experience and technique. [1] A 2012 study published by Guerrero et al. reported IAN injury incidences of 0.4 to 13.4% and LN injury incidences of 0 to 11% after third molar extractions. [7] The incidence of persistent IAN dysfunction ranged from 0 to 1.6%. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The inferior alveolar nerve is the largest branch of the mandibular nerve. It supplies sensation to the skin of the lower cheek, chin, lip, tongue, teeth, and gingivae. The IAN is an important consideration in the extraction of lower third molars, as these two structures are often closely situated. [7] As a result, there is an increased risk of damage to the IAN, which may result in a temporary or permanent altered sensation in its area of distribution. The risk of temporary altered sensation is estimated at 1% to 5%, while permanent dysaesthesia is estimated at 0 to 0.9%. [8] Cone beam computed tomography (CBCT) can provide further information on the exact relationship between a mandibular third molar and the IAN and quantify the risk of iatrogenic nerve damage. There are several radiographic features on plain film radiographs, such as orthopantomogram (OPG/DPT), which are suggestive of close proximity of a third molar to the IAN. (See figure 1). These features include:

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Technique or Treatment -- Surgical Removal
retrieved_docs.content: The socket should be irrigated and carefully debrided to sound bone, avoiding the apical aspect of the socket to prevent damage to the inferior alveolar nerve. Closure of the socket is typically achieved with resorbable sutures.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, patients on anti-coagulation medication or with bleeding disorders may present with prolonged post-operative bleeding, which will need to be managed through debridement of the socket, placement of hemostatic agents in the socket, and closure of the bleeding socket with sutures. While these complications can be seen following any dental extraction, mandibular third molars risk damage to the IAN and the lingual nerve and subsequent temporary or permanent altered sensation of the cheek, chin, lip, tongue, and teeth. Typically, paraesthesia is transient and resolves spontaneously in days, weeks, or months. If the paraesthesia is persistent after six months, it is considered permanent. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1712
retrieved_docs.content: Excision serves as a broad parent category for a variety of surgical actions, including the set of procedures labeled “Clinical actions A‑G.” Within this overarching category, many specific operative methods are classified as forms of excision: cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (both open and laparoscopic), choledochotomy or choledochostomy with duct exploration, drainage or calculus removal (with or without cholecystotomy and with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty), transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal, cervical and lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) that decompress nerve roots and may involve partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, or disc excision, radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed via a cervical approach or a sternal split approach. At the same time, the excision category explicitly excludes several laminectomy techniques used for spinal stenosis: thoracic laminectomy with cord or cauda equina decompression without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for one or two vertebral segments; and cervical, lumbar, or thoracic laminectomy for more than two vertebral segments performed under the same constraints. Excision is also linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, indicating that excisional techniques extend beyond deep organ surgery to include facial and upper aerodigestive tract interventions. This integrated view highlights excision as a versatile surgical concept that encompasses numerous specific operative methods while delineating clear boundaries for procedures it does not cover.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is classified as a clinical action within the broader group “Clinical actions A‑G” and serves as an umbrella term for a wide range of operative procedures. It includes adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, or dorsally, with or without biopsy and with possible removal of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—as well as several biliary surgeries such as cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, and associated cholangiography, which can be performed via open or laparoscopic techniques. Craniectomy or craniotomy performed exploratively in the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region also falls under Excision, as do various parathyroidectomy approaches—including cervical, sternal‑split, and transthoracic routes—each potentially combined with mediastinal exploration. In spinal surgery, Excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and excision of herniated intervertebral disc at a single lumbar level, but explicitly excludes laminectomy procedures that involve only exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for spinal stenosis across cervical and thoracic segments (both 1‑2 vertebral levels and more extensive segments). Additionally, Excision covers “other or therapeutic procedures on nose, mouth and pharynx” and is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, highlighting its relevance to both general surgical and specialized reconstructive interventions. This comprehensive definition clarifies the scope of Excision, distinguishing it from specific laminectomy techniques while integrating diverse surgical modalities under a single procedural category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1879
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical category that includes a wide variety of operative techniques and is positioned within the larger group of Clinical actions A‑G. It encompasses arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from joints, multiple forms of cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (including choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy, and cholangiography), and cranial procedures such as exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region and suboccipital craniectomy for cranial‑nerve decompression. Excision also covers orbital exploration by a transcranial approach, cervical and lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, foraminotomy, partial facetectomy, and disc excision, as well as nipple exploration with lactiferous‑duct excision. Parathyroid surgery falls under this umbrella, including standard parathyroidectomy, parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via sternal split or transthoracic approaches, and combined mediastinal‑exploration procedures. In addition to these methods, Excision is linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx and maintains a specific relationship with plastic procedures on the nose. Certain extensive spinal procedures—such as multisegment thoracic laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy—are explicitly excluded from the Excision category. Together, these relationships define Excision as a versatile operative concept that integrates numerous specialized techniques while delineating its scope relative to broader clinical actions and excluded methods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2756
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is presented as a broad surgical category that includes a wide variety of organ‑specific and region‑specific procedures while explicitly excluding certain extensive spinal decompressions. Within this category, adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, and sometimes accompanied by removal of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—uses an excision method. Cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, either with cholangiography or with choledochoenterostomy, is also classified as an excision technique. Exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region falls under the same umbrella. In the spine, excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and removal of herniated intervertebral disc when performed at a single interspace in the cervical or lumbar spine. By contrast, more extensive laminectomy procedures that decompress the spinal cord or cauda equina over more than two vertebral segments—whether cervical, lumbar, or thoracic—are specifically listed as excluded methods for excision. Other procedures grouped under excision include nipple exploration with lactiferous‑duct excision, parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration, and parathyroidectomy that involves mediastinal exploration via a sternal split approach. Excision is positioned within the higher‑level grouping “Clinical actions A‑G,” indicating that it is one of the clinical actions encompassed by that classification. Additionally, excision is linked to plastic procedures on the nose and is identified as a method within the broader set of “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx,” reflecting its relevance to reconstructive and therapeutic interventions in those anatomical regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2167
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical category that falls under the parent group “Clinical actions A‑G.” It includes a wide range of operative methods such as partial or complete adrenalectomy (with or without biopsy) performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches; cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct, both with and without cholangiography; choledochotomy or choledochostomy with duct exploration, drainage, or calculus removal, optionally combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty; transcranial exploration of the orbit with lesion removal; laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and disc excision in a single lumbar interspace; laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration; radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration; and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed via cervical or transthoracic approaches. The Excision category explicitly excludes certain laminectomy procedures: lumbar laminectomy with exploration and decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina that involves more than two vertebral segments, and cervical laminectomy with similar exploration that does not include facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy. In addition to these operative techniques, Excision is linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx and is related to plastic procedures on the nose. This integrated framework illustrates how Excision groups diverse anatomical surgeries while defining specific procedural exclusions and connections to related therapeutic domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2787
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is presented as a broad surgical category that serves as the parent concept for Clinical actions A – G and is identified as the answer to the Chinese‑language diagnostic phrase “结直肠癌主要诊断的程序:发现:时间点:^患者:名义型:PhenX,” linking it to colorectal cancer diagnostic processes. Within the Excision hierarchy, a wide array of specific operative methods are enumerated, including Laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord and/or cauda equina (excluding procedures that involve more than two cervical vertebral segments), several variants of cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct (with or without cholangiography, transduodenal sphincterotomy, or sphincteroplasty), Choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage or calculus removal, cranial procedures such as infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy, laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration, nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct, and multiple forms of parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration (including approaches with mediastinal exploration, sternal split, or transthoracic access). The category also designates “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx” as a method of Excision and records a relationship to plastic procedures on the nose, indicating its relevance to both therapeutic and reconstructive interventions in the facial region. This integrated view underscores Excision as a versatile procedural framework encompassing diverse operative techniques, specific exclusions, and connections to both diagnostic pathways and plastic surgical applications.
